questions.question: Define the Critical Section problem and state its three essential requirements.
keyPoints: Critical Section: Code segment accessing shared resources
Mutual Exclusion: Only one process in CS at a time
Progress: Selection made if CS free and processes want entry
Bounded Waiting: Limit on entries after request before process gets chance

questions.question: Explain the concept of a Race Condition with an example.
keyPoints: Race Condition: Outcome depends on timing of interleaved operations
Example: Two processes incrementing shared variable counter
If both read value 5 simultaneously, both write 6, losing one increment
Results in incorrect final value due to lack of synchronization

questions.question: Describe the Test-and-Set instruction for achieving mutual exclusion.
keyPoints: Hardware instruction providing atomic read-modify-write
Reads current lock value, returns it, and sets lock to true in one operation
Process checks returned value - if false, lock acquired; if true, wait and retry
Enables implementation of spinlocks for mutual exclusion

questions.question: What is a Semaphore? Differentiate between Binary and Counting semaphores.
keyPoints: Semaphore: Synchronization variable with integer value and atomic wait/signal operations
Binary Semaphore: Value 0 or 1, used for mutual exclusion (like mutex)
Counting Semaphore: Value 0 to N, represents available instances of resource
Initial value equals number of resources for counting semaphore

questions.question: Explain the difference between Busy Waiting (Spinlock) and Blocking Wait in process synchronization.
keyPoints: Busy Waiting: Process continuously checks lock variable in loop, wastes CPU cycles
Blocking Wait: Process is put to sleep/blocked, added to queue, awakened when lock available
Spinlocks suitable for short waits, multiprocessor systems
Blocking wait efficient for long critical sections, single CPU systems

questions.question: What is the difference between Deadlock and Starvation?

explain.keyDifference: Deadlock: Circular wait with all processes blocked. Starvation: Process perpetually denied resource due to unfair scheduling.

keyPoints: Deadlock: Processes blocked indefinitely, waiting for each other
Starvation: Ready process never gets CPU due to priority or scheduling
Bounded waiting in CS prevents starvation but not deadlock
Deadlock involves all processes blocked; starvation can involve one process

questions.question: Explain the Monitor construct in process synchronization.
keyPoints: Monitor: High-level language synchronization construct
Combines shared data with procedures operating on that data
Provides automatic mutual exclusion - only one process can execute inside monitor
Contains condition variables for waiting and signaling between processes

questions.question: Explain Peterson's solution for the Critical Section problem for two processes. Also explain why it satisfies all three requirements.
keyPoints: Two shared variables: 'turn' and 'flag[2]','flag[i]=true means process i wants to enter CS
Entry section: Process sets flag, sets turn to other process, then waits while other wants CS and it's other's turn
Exit section: Process sets flag to false
Mutual Exclusion: Both cannot pass entry condition simultaneously
Progress: If only one wants, it enters; if both want, one waits based on turn
Bounded Waiting: After process requests, other can enter at most once before requester gets turn

questions.question: Explain the Critical Section problem and describe any two hardware-based solutions for achieving mutual exclusion.
keyPoints: CS Problem: Coordinate multiple processes accessing shared resources
Hardware Solutions:
1. Disable Interrupts: Prevents context switch, but not suitable for multiprocessor
2. Test-and-Set: Atomic instruction, returns old value while setting to true
3. Compare-and-Swap: Compares memory with expected, swaps if matches
4. Exchange/Swap: Atomically exchanges values of two variables
Hardware solutions simpler but can cause busy waiting
Each provides atomic operations impossible to implement in software alone

questions.question: What are semaphores? Explain the wait() and signal() operations. How are they used for solving the Critical Section problem?
keyPoints: Semaphore: Integer variable with two atomic operations
wait(): Decrement value, block if becomes negative
signal(): Increment value, wake waiting process if any
For Mutual Exclusion: Initialize binary semaphore to 1
Process does wait() before CS, signal() after CS
If semaphore is 0 (CS occupied), process blocks
Ensures only one process in CS at any time

questions.question: Discuss the differences between Software Solutions, Hardware Solutions, and Language Constructs for process synchronization. Give examples of each.
keyPoints: Software Solutions: Implemented purely in code using shared variables
Examples: Peterson's Algorithm, Dekker's Algorithm
Disadvantage: Complex, only work for limited processes, require careful design
Hardware Solutions: Use special CPU instructions
Examples: Test-and-Set, Compare-and-Swap, Exchange
Advantage: Simple, efficient; Disadvantage: Busy waiting possible
Language Constructs: High-level synchronization abstractions
Examples: Monitors, Conditional Critical Regions
Advantage: Cleaner, automatic enforcement; Disadvantage: Language support needed
